coredata.dc:title: Detection, detoxification, and anti-counterfeiting applications of pullulan-modified nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Tammina, Sai Kumar
Vinoth Kumar, Jothi
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) modified with pullulan were prepared using a hydrothermal method for use in sensing, detoxification, and anti-counterfeiting applications. The excitation-independent N-CDs demonstrated selectivity for hydroquinone and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) with detection limits of 58 and 13 nM. The N-CD-based detection method was extended to environmental samples such as bottled water, tap water, and river water, and acceptable relative standard deviations (RSDs) and good recoveries were found. In the presence of NaBH4, N-CDs converted the toxic MB to colorless leuco-methylene blue in 10.5 min, confirming that the catalytic ability of N-CDs can be evaluated using MB as a model pollutant. With an excellent quantum yield of 31 %, the N-CDs had the potential as a fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting applications by helping to visualize fingerprints, drawings, and chemical structures under 365 nm UV light while being completely invisible in daylight and sunlight. The N-CDs have significant potential for industrial use as fluorescent inks, detoxification catalysts, and pollutant sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pullulan
N-CDs
Hydroquinone
4-Nitrophenol
MB reduction
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of N-CDs against 4-NP and HQ
Influence of pH on sensing HQ and 4-NP
Plausible reduction mechanism for MB decolorization

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2022R1A2B5B02001422).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: N-CDs structural characterization. (a) X-ray diffraction, (b) survey spectrum, deconvoluted spectra of (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) O 1s, and (f) FT-IR spectrum.
(a) TEM image (20 nm), (b) HR-TEM image (2 nm), and (b) size distribution of N-CDs.
(a) Emission of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (b) excitation and emission spectra (c) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs.
(a) Selectivity of N-CDs, interferents evaluation for N-CDs against (b) 4-NP and (c) HQ.
Emission response of N-CDs against (a) 4-NP and (c) HQ, and linear plot between the concentration of (b) 4-NP and (d) HQ.
Absorption spectra of N-CDs in the presence of (a) 4-NP and (c) HQ, and overlay of (b) 4-NP and (d) HQ absorbance and excitation spectra of N-CDs.
Stern-Volmer linear plot of N-CDs in the presence of (a) 4-NP and (b) HQ.
Influence of zeta potential on (a) N-CDs in the presence of (b) 4-NP and (c) HQ.
Effect of pH on N-CD in the absence and presence of 4-NP and HQ.
TRPL titrations of N-CDs in the presence of (a) 4-NP and (b) HQ.
(a) Absorbance changes of MB in the absence of (a) N-CDs and (b) N-CDs, and (c) linear plot of pseudo-first-order kinetics with N-CDs and MB.
Drawings and writings drawn with N-CDs on Whatman (No. 42) filter paper, exposed to daylight and 365 nm light.
Schematic illustration of reduction of methylene blue in the presence of N-CDs and NaBH4.
Comparison of properties of polymer-based carbon dots.
Influence of 4-NP and HQ concentration on PLQY of N-CDs.
Faster (τ1 and τ2), slower (τ3), and average (τavg) decay values of N-CDs in 4-NP(μM) solution.
Faster (τ1 and τ2), slower (τ3), and average (τavg) decay values of N-CDs in HQ (μM) solution.
Real sample analysis for 4-NP in tap, drinking, and river water (n = 3).
Real sample analysis for HQ in tap, drinking, and river water (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Detection, detoxification, and anti-counterfeiting applications of pullulan-modified nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, Seoul, 02447, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, Seoul, 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) modified with pullulan were prepared using a hydrothermal method for use in sensing, detoxification, and anti-counterfeiting applications. The excitation-independent N-CDs demonstrated selectivity for hydroquinone and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) with detection limits of 58 and 13 nM. The N-CD-based detection method was extended to environmental samples such as bottled water, tap water, and river water, and acceptable relative standard deviations (RSDs) and good recoveries were found. In the presence of NaBH4, N-CDs converted the toxic MB to colorless leuco-methylene blue in 10.5 min, confirming that the catalytic ability of N-CDs can be evaluated using MB as a model pollutant. With an excellent quantum yield of 31 %, the N-CDs had the potential as a fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting applications by helping to visualize fingerprints, drawings, and chemical structures under 365 nm UV light while being completely invisible in daylight and sunlight. The N-CDs have significant potential for industrial use as fluorescent inks, detoxification catalysts, and pollutant sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, there has been increasing interest in risk assessment of pollutants in various industrial fields and their impact on human life [1,2]. Among the main raw materials and by-products of numerous chemical manufacturing processes are various phenolic compounds, many of which are discarded in nature and are resistant to biological degradation and highly toxic [3]. Among these phenolic compounds, 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) and hydroquinone (HQ), it is one of the major water pollutants in aquatic systems. 4-NP is a nitroaromatic chemical used in a variety of applications as a precursor or intermediate for the manufacture of explosives, pesticides, pigments, anti-corrosion lubricants, wood preservatives, and pharmaceuticals. Wastewater discharged from these industries pollutes many freshwater lakes, and its high solubility and chemical stability in water affect human health as well as surrounding environmental conditions [4]. HQ (1,4-dihydroxybenzene), a well-known phenolic molecule, has been widely used to make a variety of chemical products, including industrial agents, pharmaceuticals, and pesticides. As a result, we are present in a variety of industrial wastewater, including rubber, leather, plastics, and oil refineries [5]. It should be mentioned that the intractability of this substance can lead to the development of leukemia, damage to some organs, including the kidneys, and increased accumulation of toxicity in the body. Therefore, the headquarters has the potential to cause ecological imbalance, environmental damage, and, ultimately, human health problems [6]. Therefore, to detect 4-NP and HQ with both selectivity and sensitivity, a simple, reliable, and fast analytical method must be developed. Several techniques were developed to determine the 4-NP and HQ concentration, such as colorimetry [7,8], electrochemical [9,10], GC-MS [11,12], LC-MS [13,14], and HPLC [15,16]. Among these, the fluorescence approach has an advantage over other methods as it can detect 4-NP and HQ in a variety of samples quickly, easily, and inexpensively.

In addition, organic dyes are widely known to be widely used in various industries such as food, medicine, cosmetics, paint, plastic, paper, and textile. However, synthetic dyes have been determined to be dangerous not only to human health but also to the natural world. Many traditional treatment techniques, including filtration, adsorption, coagulation, and reverse osmosis, have been used to remove dyes and discolor them from wastewater. However, it has proven difficult to remove from wastewater due to its persistent aromatic content. Nanocatalyst is one of the reagents recently used to perform reductive discoloration of dyes [17–19].

The security and invisible labeling of information are becoming essential to combat counterfeiting for many government agencies, corporate brands, and industrial sectors. A variety of secure encryption methods are currently being used to protect and transmit sensitive data, including RFID, laser holograms, and quick response codes (QR codes). Due to its fast response time, excellent stability, and intuitive design, optical technology is more effective in this specific application than other approaches. Fluorescent substances based on lanthanide elements, semiconductor quantum dots, and organic fluorescent dyes are used in the composition of many fluorescent labeling products. However, significant drawbacks, including environmental toxicity and heavy metal emissions, limit their use in various industries. Recently, carbon dot-based fluorescent ink has become popular for use in secret code exchange, security code encryption, and anti-counterfeiting applications due to their low photo-bleaching effect, low toxicity, high emission of fluorescence, high stability, and ease of processing [20,21]. Therefore, it is important to prepare highly fluorescent carbon dots for anti-counterfeiting applications.

Fluorescence is the measurement of the change in emission of a fluorescent substance in the presence of a target analyte. A variety of emissive nanomaterials have been reported, including CdSe QDs, MOFs, GQDs, and CQDs. All of these materials have been reported as fluorescence sensors. However, among all CQDs, the carbon source of CQDs is cheaper in terms of manufacturing and it is easier to tune the surface and emission properties with a cost-effective strategy because several green natural sources help to tune the properties by surface passivation. Additionally, the application of CQD is not limited to detection. Their potential has been explored in several other applications, including photocatalysis, photovoltaics, food packaging, anti-counterfeiting, catalysis, and biomedical applications.

Although research into CD applications has been ongoing for decades, there are still several challenges to overcome, including low quantum yield, low detection limits, and particle aggregation upon long-term storage. Additionally, the use of different carbon sources should be explored to generate cost-effective CDs with promising antibacterial, antioxidant, and stable fluorescent materials for food packaging and sensing applications.

There are several reports on the synthesis of CDs using citric acid and ethylenediamine with different quantum yields depending on the concentration and synthetic approach for several applications, including sensing and biomedical applications [22–26]. Most of these CDs are utilized in sensing and biomedical applications. However, the versatility of CDs depends purely on their surface functionalization, and the chemical reactivity and fluorescence properties of CDs vary with respect to the surface precursors used for their synthesis. Using identical moieties can achieve similar affinities for similar target analytes. Therefore, dopants or additional precursors must be added to bring about the required diversity in terms of emission, stability, and surface properties of CDs. In previous investigations, we observed that capping or tuning surfaces using precursors can provide photostability and biocompatibility along with varying affinities for analytes [23,27,28]. In this study, pullulan was included in the synthesis process to impart new fluorescence properties to the carbon dots. A nonionic water-soluble linear polysaccharide known as pullulan is composed of α-1,6-linked malt trisaccharides and is released by a yeast-like mold called Aureobasidium pullulans species. Due to the excellent biocompatibility, non-immunogenic, non-mutagenic, non-carcinogenic, and biodegradable properties of pullulan, pullulan-stabilized nanoparticles are useful for cytotoxicity, therapeutic, and drug delivery studies [29]. Hossam et al. reported the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs with a combination of carrageenan and pullulan molecules and evaluated their anticancer and antiviral activities [30]. However, to the best of our knowledge, no reports are indicating the development of environmental pollutant sensors using pullulan-stabilized CDs.

Therefore, we explored the potential of hydrothermally synthesized CDs using citric acid, pullulan, and ethylenediamine precursors for the detection of water contaminants such as 4-NP and HQ, catalytic reduction of methylene blue, and anti-counterfeiting properties of CDs. Moreover, the N-CDs only responded to HQ, regardless of interference. The main goal of this work was to develop a functionalized CD-based spectrofluorometric method for HQ and 4-NP detection that is reasonably easy to use, selective, sensitive, and inexpensive.

Ethylenediamine was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Buffer solutions ranging from pH 2 to 12 and citric acid were procured from Daejeong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Siheung, Gyeonggi-do, Korea). Pullulan was purchased from Korea Bio Polymer Co. Ltd. (Bucheon, Gyeonggi-do, Korea).

Nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) were prepared using a hydrothermal method. For this purpose, 1 g of pullulan, 5 mL of ethylenediamine, and 0.9 g of citric acid were dissolved in 50 mL of water, then transferred to a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor lined with Teflon and reacted at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling the reaction solution to room temperature, the reaction mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove unreacted substances. The brown supernatant was filtered through a membrane filter (pore size 0.22 μM). The filtrate was lyophilized to obtain a black powder of CD and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C before further testing.

The XRD patterns of the N-CDs were collected using a Panalytical X'pert proMRD diffractometer (Amsterdam, Netherlands) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1541 nm) with an incident X-ray source (40 kV, 300 mA) in the range of 2θ = 5–80° at a scan speed of 0.4° min−1. The functional groups on the surface of N-CDs were analyzed using an attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectrophotometer (TENSOR 37 Spectrophotometer, Billerica, MA, USA) in the range of 4000-500 cm−1 with an average resolution of 32 scans at 4 cm−1. The chemical composition of N-CDs was evaluated using a K-alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Scientific Co. Ltd., Waltham, MA, USA). The optical absorption properties of the N-CDs in aqueous solution were evaluated using a UV–Vis optical absorption spectrophotometer (Mecasys Optizen POP series, Seoul) at 200–800 nm. The fluorescence properties of the N-CDs in an aqueous solution were evaluated using a 1 cm square quartz cuvette in a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7100, Japan, xenon lamp, PMT voltage: 400 V, bandwidth: 0.5 nm). Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering were analyzed using a zeta sizer (Nano ZS-ZEN 3600, Malvern Instruments Ltd, Malvern, Worcestershire, UK) to measure the surface charge and particle size distribution of the particles. A JEM-2100F FE-TEM (field emission-transmission electron microscope, Jeol Ltd, Tokyo, Japan) was used to evaluate the size and morphology of the N-CDs. The average particle size and particle size distribution were calculated using the ImageJ program. An FP-8500 spectrophotometer (Jasco, Japan) was used to measure the PLQY of the N-CDs.

After adding 75 μL of N-CDs solution to 3 mL of water, different analyte concentrations (HQ or 4-NP) were added, and the mixture was made to a 4 mL volume using distilled water. The liquid was vortexed for 30 s and then incubated for 15 min to ensure uniform mixing. Analyte solutions were subjected to spectrophotometric analysis using a fluorescence spectrophotometer after an incubation period of 15 min.

To explore the feasibility of the developed N-CD detection method, an analysis of real samples of river, tap water, and drinking water was performed. Three types of water samples were used: tap water, bottled water, and river water. Samples were filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove larger particles. For the real sample analysis, appropriate amounts of 4-NP and HQ were added to the filtered samples at pH 8. Emission changes were measured after incubation for 10 min using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The discharge data were then used to calculate percent recovery and relative standard deviation.

The methylene blue (MB) reduction reaction was performed in a 3.5 mL quartz cuvette. Several studies have been performed to determine the optimal concentrations of MB, NaBH4, and N-CDs. In the first reaction, 1.50 mL of MB (1 × 10−4 M) was mixed with 0.20 mL of freshly prepared ice-cold NaBH4 (0.50 M) and 1.30 mL of water. In the second reaction, 25 μL of prepared N-CDs was added to the reaction mixture, and these reactions were continuously monitored at linear time intervals using a UV–vis spectrophotometer.

Random biopolymer chemical structures and figures were drawn using a fountain pen filled with diluted N-CD solution on Whatman filter paper. The filter papers were then dried at ambient temperature and examined in an ultraviolet chamber equipped with a radiation source emitting light at 254 and 365 nm.

The X-ray diffraction pattern of the synthesized N-CDs is shown in Fig. 1 a, which shows diffraction at 23°, consistent with the graphite plane (002). Additionally, the weak diffraction at 43° and 63.5° may correspond to a stack of typical graphite interlayer diffraction (001), indicating the formation of a graphene-like carbon core. The broad appearance of the diffraction indicates the disordered nature of the carbon atoms in the crystal lattice [31,32].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to confirm further the surface chemical structure of the N-CDs. The survey spectrum (Fig. 1b) of XPS showed the binding energies of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s at 285.1 eV, 398.9 eV, and 531.14 eV with an atomic percentage of 63.1, 15.4, and 21.5 %, respectively, confirming the presence of functional moieties of C, N, and O elements on the surface of N-CDs. For further analysis of functional group identification, the deconvoluted spectra, in which the binding energy of each element was examined separately, are shown in Fig. 1c–e. The C 1s deconvoluted spectrum displayed the binding energies at 283.84 and 285.11 eV corresponding to the C–C/CC, and 286.75 and 287.74 eV binding energies are due to the C–O/C–N and CO/CN, respectively [33]. N 1s deconvoluted spectrum displayed corresponding pyridinic-like nitrogen and pyrrolic-like nitrogen binding energies at 398.9, 398.3, and 399 eV, respectively [32]. The oxygen element binding energies are found at 529 and 530.4 eV, indicating the presence of C–O and CO groups on the N-CD surface [33]. This analysis confirms the presence of functional moieties associated with the C, N, and O groups on the surface, which may aid in interaction with the target analyte.

The structural information of N-CDs was further evaluated through FTIR, and the results are shown in Fig. 1f. The stretching vibrations at 2922 and 2868 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the C–H group of the ethylenediamine moiety. The carboxyl O–H stretching and bending vibrations were observed at 3284 and 1471 cm−1, respectively. Additionally, the vibration at 1428 cm−1 is attributed to alcoholic O–H, and the vibration frequency associated with the CO group is found at 1652 cm−1. The bending vibration at 1556 cm−1 is attributed to the N–H part. Also, the absorptions at 1151, 1087, and 1022 cm−1 may correspond to C–N, C–O, and C–O–C vibrations. Structural properties and their discussion show that the synthesized carbon dots are amorphous with abundant hydroxyl, amine, and carboxyl groups.

Morphological analysis of N-CDs was evaluated by TEM (Fig. 2 ). The particles are distributed without aggregation (Fig. 2a), and the HR-TEM image illustrates the quasi-spherical shape of N-CDs (Fig. 2b). The size of the particles was measured using ImageJ software, and 100 particles were considered for the measurement. Most particles were in the size range of 2–8 nm, with an average diameter of 3.8 ± 0.6 nm (Fig. 2c).

The emission and absorption properties of N-CDs were evaluated via fluorescence and UV–Vis spectrophotometry. Fig. 3 shows the emission, excitation, and absorption spectra of N-CDs, where the emission changes were recorded by varying the excitation wavelength from 264 nm to 440 nm (Fig. 3a). The emission intensity of N-CD increased with increasing excitation wavelength, and the maximum emission intensity was observed at 396 nm excitation with an emission wavelength of 453 nm. Here, changes with respect to the excitation wavelength were observed only in the emission intensity at a wavelength of 453 nm, but there was no significant change in the emission wavelength, confirming the excitation-independent behavior of N-CD. It might be due to the particle size distribution and surface/molecular state of N-CDs being responsible for this phenomenon [34]. Changes in emission are visible only at higher wavelengths, especially at 400 nm. Passivation defects and similar types of functional groups present on the surface with uniform size distribution may be responsible for this small excitation dependence at higher excitation wavelengths [35]. In particular, the excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs (Fig. 3b) displayed three excitation peaks at 264, 314, and 396 nm with a single emission at 453 nm, which is different from conventional carbon dots. Chen et al. reported that the intrinsic energy levels of the surface bands, the n-π* edge transition, and the π-π* transition of the core can serve as the fluorescence center of N-CD [36]. It is speculated that the interaction between the sp2 core of N-CDs and the surface functional groups may be responsible for the multiple excitation and single-color emission phenomena [37].

CDs were synthesized in the absence of pullulan under similar experimental conditions to evaluate the role of pullulan on the emission properties of N-CDs,. The observed changes are shown in Fig. S1. In the absence of pullulan, a change in emission intensity was observed as the excitation wavelength increased from 260 to 440 nm (Fig. S1a). The excitation spectrum of CD displayed two bands at 276 and 402 nm, and the corresponding emissions were observed at 439 and 443 nm (Fig. S1b). However, the emission wavelength did not change at different wavelengths, demonstrating excitation-independent emission of CDs that is comparable to the behavior of NCDs. The maximum emission intensity was observed at 443 nm with an excitation wavelength of 402 nm (Fig. S1a), and the excitation spectra displayed two bands at 276 nm along with 402 nm, so the emission intensity for 276 nm excitation was observed at 439 nm. Unlike N-CD, CD showed only two excitations (276 and 402 nm). Interestingly, in CD, a blue shift in emission was observed in the absence of pullulan. The difference in emission wavelength of CD (443 nm) and N-CD (453 nm) was 10 nm, and the presence of pullulan caused a red shift in the emission, indicating that it supports the wavelength shift of N-CD to longer wavelengths. It may have originated from conjugation, but conjugation may have been involved in conjugation due to the polymer structure, bringing the electronic transition energy to longer wavelengths. The presence of conjugation results in a redshift of the N-CD emission.

Additionally, in the presence of pullulan, an additional absorption band is found at 314 nm (Fig. 3b), which helps N-CD to absorb maximum UV radiation and can be a UV-blocking material for various applications. For example, the packaging industry requires UV-blocking materials to prevent spoilage of materials due to UV radiation. For example, food packaging films require UV-blocking properties because food becomes rancid under UV irradiation [27,38,39], so they can be useful UV-blocking materials for food packaging applications. Additionally, the drug packaging industry desperately needs UV-blocking materials to help prevent structural changes caused by UV radiation in the case of UV-sensitive drugs [40]. The textile industry is also making UV-blocking clothing to protect people from skin cancer [41]. The red shift of the emission behavior and the additional UV absorption band to aid the UV blocking behavior show that the addition of pullulan can bring about significant changes that are useful for food and pharmaceutical packaging applications.

According to the fluorescence results, the optical properties were further evaluated using UV–Vis spectrophotometry (Fig. 3c). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum displayed three absorption bands at 240, 287, and 352 nm, corresponding to π-π*, n-σ* from CC of sp2 core structure, n-σ* from amino as well as hydroxyl groups and n-π* transitions from the carboxyl groups [37]. Additionally, the quantum yield of N-CDs was found to be 31 %, which is a promising ratio and is expected to be well-suited for detection and anti-counterfeiting applications. Table 1 shows recent literature on the comparison of polymer-based CD synthesis methods, properties, and applications. The currently reported N-CD properties are almost similar to those in the literature, but their applications differ. Most CDs reported emission in the green region, especially 450–470 nm, except for carboxymethyl cellulose-based CDs. Additionally, the size of each type of CD is reported to be less than 5 nm, including the current report. Quantum yields of 23 % have not been reported for any other CD except hemicellulose-based CDs, and current reports are almost identical, showing that the properties of pullulan-based CDs are similar to the results in the existing literature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of N-CDs against 4-NP and HQ
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response of N-CDs to various analytes was evaluated using fluorescence spectrophotometry (Fig. 4 ). For the selectivity evaluation, several analytes were used, including 4-NP, 2-NP, HQ (hydroquinone), Res (resorcinol), CTC (catechol), BPA (bisphenol A), OPD (O-phenylenediamine), AA (ascorbic acid), Glu (glucose), Zn(II), Pb(II), Ni(II), Na(I), Mg(II), Mn(VI), K(I), Hg(II), Fe(II), Fe(III), Cu(II), Co(II), Mg(II), Ce(III), Ca(II), Bi(III), Au(III), and Ag(I). All of these analytes were added at a concentration of 25 μM, and this release response is shown in Fig. 4a. Among all analytes, N-CDs showed a significant response to HQ and 4-NP. Although the reason for the specific response to these analytes is unknown, further evaluation may reveal the reason for the inhibitory response of N-CDs to 4-NP and HQ. Interfering substance investigations were also performed to evaluate the potential detection ability in the presence of other analytes. However, the developed fluorescent sensor showed detection potential when different analyte concentrations were present in a 1:1 ratio (Fig. 4b and c)

When titrating N-CDs using various concentrations of 4-NP (0–250 μM) (Fig. 5 a), the release response was gradually quenched with increasing concentration, and at higher concentrations of 4-NP, no further release occurred. There was a linear relationship between F/Fo and 4-NP concentration with a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.992, as shown in eqn. (1).
(1)
y = 0.0229x + 0.9873 (R2 = 0.992)

The detection limit of N-CDs for 4-NP was calculated using 3σ/k, where σ represents the standard deviation of the blank (n = 11) and "k" represents the slope value. The detection limit was found at 13 nM, which is comparable to the existing updated report.

Similarly, as the concentration of HQ increased, the release was gradually quenched (Fig. 5c), and the release quenching response was significant but relatively less than that of 4-NP, which may be a difference in the interaction of HQ and N-CDs compared to 4-NP. However, there was a linear relationship between F/Fo and HQ concentration with a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.98, as shown in eqn. (2) (Fig. 5d).
(2)
y = 0.0051x + 0.991 (R2 = 0.98)

The detection limit for this titration was observed at 58 nM, which is relatively compatible with the reported detection limit of the fluorescent probe. The results of titration, discussion, and comparison demonstrate that the synthesized N-CDs are a promising fluorescent sensor for detecting analytes in the nanomolar concentration range, and the practicality of the developed detection strategy is further evaluated in real sample settings. However, the reason for the emission suppression of N-CDs needs to be analyzed, and the detection mechanism is discussed in the next section.

The UV–Vis absorption patterns were recorded to gain deep insight into the actual quenching reaction of N-CDs in the presence of analytes (Fig. 6 ). As the concentration of 4-NP increased, the absorption at 284 nm and 350 nm continued to increase. These two transitions are related to the n-π* and π-π* transitions of N-CDs (Fig. 6a), which shows that absorption attenuation occurred when 4-NP was added to N-CDs. Even the excitation spectrum of N-CDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of 4-NP (Fig. 6b). However, after a certain concentration of 4-NP, especially 100 μM concentration, a new absorption band appeared, and at 250 μM concentration of 4-NP, a new absorption band was visible at 410 nm, which may be due to the phenolate ion of 4-NP. The peak of the phenolate ion comes at 410 nm. For HQ, as the concentration of HQ gradually increased in the presence of N-CDs, the electronic transition associated with N-CDs continued to increase up to a concentration of 250 μM (Fig. 6c). Even the absorption spectrum of HQ overlaps with N-CD excitation (Fig. 6d). The above discussion supports the internal filter effect phenomenon, and further evaluation continues to gain deeper insights.

Additionally, the Stern-Volmer analysis provided additional insight into the quenching effect. Linear regression with a fixed intercept of 1 shows the relative fluorescence response to 4-NP and HQ concentrations (Fig. 7 a& b).
(3)
I o
I
= 1 +
k
s v
[ Q ]

K
SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and I
o and I are the emission intensity of N-CDs in the absence and presence of various concentrations of 4-NP and HQ, respectively. The molar concentration of the analyte is denoted by Q. The calculated quenching contents are 4.7 × 104 M−1 and 0.95 × 104 M−1 for 4-NP and HQ, respectively. Differences in quenching constant values indicate differences in the solubility of the quencher to the fluorophore [48]. In the presence of 4-NP, the higher Ksv constant of N-CDs indicates the high solubility of 4-NP in the excited state, which may significantly reduce the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorophore. However, for HQ, the complex can be formed in the ground state, and the Ksv constant value indicates a significant distance between HQ and N-CDs. This situation may not reduce fluorescence lifetime, which can be static quenching, and 4-NP can cause dynamic quenching. TRPL titration data can further support this.

The influence of 4-NP and HQ on the N-CDs zeta potential was evaluated to determine the interaction between the analyte and the fluorescent probe. As shown in Fig. 8 a, N-CDs exhibited a zeta potential of −3.29 ± 2.5 mV. However, after the addition of 4-NPs, interaction or accumulation of 4-NPs occurred on the surface, increasing the zeta potential of N-CDs (14.03 ± 1.5 mV) (Fig. 8b). In the presence of HQ, N-CDs were also increased (15.56 ± 3.38 mV) (Fig. 8 c), indicating a rise in N-CDs surface charge and supporting the surface-tailored identification of 4-NP and HQ by the negatively charged surface of N-CDs. This discussion shows that the presence of both analytes has a significant impact on the surface environment of N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH on sensing HQ and 4-NP
ce:section.ce:para: To find the optimal pH conditions to perform the sensing activity, the effect of pH on the fluorescence emission of N-CDs and the quenching behavior of N-CDs in the presence of both analytes was investigated (Fig. 9 ). The pH effect was investigated from 2 pH conditions to 12 pH conditions, and regardless of pH, N-CDs can exhibit significantly good release properties. There was no significant release inhibition at all pH conditions. However, after adding the analytes, there were differences in the release properties, especially in the presence of HQ 4-NPs, which could not bring about differences in the release properties of N-CDs, and the release suppression continued regardless of pH condition. However, inhibition of release did not occur in the presence of HQ under low pH conditions, especially at pH 3–7, which may be due to differences in dissociation from HQ to 4-NP. The dissociation constant of HQ is 9.9, which is higher than that of 4-NP (7.15). Dissociation of HQ may occur at higher pH conditions, which may be the reason why N-CDs do not show inhibition of release in the presence of HQ. Dissociation or protonation of hydroxyl groups may not occur under low pH conditions. There may have been repulsion between the functional or surface groups of N-CDs and HQ, and the interaction may have occurred at higher pH conditions, showing that designing sensors for HQ requires fluorescent materials with high pH stability.

To further investigate the sensing mechanism, quantum yield titrations were performed on 4-NP and HQ at different concentrations in the presence and absence of N-CDs. Table 2 shows the effect of analytes on N-CD QY. Here, the QY of N-CDs was reduced with immediate effect in the presence of both analytes. The absorption efficiency of N-CDs decreased significantly with increasing concentrations of both analytes. Interestingly, the reduction in QY was less with HQ (11.2 %) than with 4-NP (2.1 %). This may be due to the difference in absorption attenuation of N-CDs in the presence of HQ and 4-NP due to the different electronic transitions of the two analytes. The overlap of the energy absorption ranges of 4-NP can be relatively higher than that of HQ using the N-CDs electronic transition spectrum. This may be the reason why the LOD of HQ was lower than that of 4-NP in the current investigation.

However, in the case of HQ, the lifetime of the fluorophore does not drop and is still the same at higher concentrations (Table 4), and complex formation in the ground state may be the reason for the fluorescence quenching of the fluorophore. This shows that static quenching and the absorption spectra of N-CDs and HQ complexes almost overlap (Fig. 6 d), indicating that the internal filter effect may be an additional mechanism for suppressing N-CD fluorescence [49]. The overall discussion reveals that N-CDs are involved in both dynamic and static quenching and emission quenching of IFE concerning 4-NP and HQ.

The N-CDs release responses for real samples spiked with various concentrations of 4-NP and HQ are presented in Tables 5 and 6
, respectively. Examining the durability of N-CDs as a photoluminescence probe for the identification of spiked 4-NP and HQ in real water samples was performed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. It is worth noting that actual water samples do not contain 4-NP or HQ concentrations. However, %RSD and recovery were found to be within acceptable ranges with good accuracy and precision, regardless of actual sample type and analyte. This result shows that the proposed detection method can be suitable for detecting 4-NP and HQ in real samples.

Methylene blue (MB) is a type of cationic dye that is easily soluble in water and is mainly used in the fields of food coloring, textiles, packaging, and paper. MB is used to analyze sulfide ions in real water samples and as a cellular imaging agent for colorants [31]. It causes cancer, skin irritation, aquaculture, eye burns, mutagenesis, microbiology, surgery, allergic reactions, and diagnostic applications in both humans and animals. MB has an absorption peak at 664 nm, and the reduction was performed in a 3.0 mL cuvette. 2.0 mL of dye, 0.20 mL of NaBH4, and 1 mL of water were introduced into this reduction reaction (without N-CDs), and there was a slight decrease in intensity after 90 min (Fig. 11 a). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of MB reduction with NaBH4 in the presence of nanocatalyst N-CDs (10 μL) showed linear reduction at regular time intervals, and the reaction was completed within 10.5 min, as shown in Fig. 11b.

Additionally, the color of the dye solution changed to colorless. Typically, the oxidized form of MB appears blue, while the reduced form, called leuco-methylene blue, appears colorless. Using the change in absorbance of MB over time, the coefficient of determination (R2) and rate constant (k) values were derived from linear fit. The R2 and rate constant (k) values of the linear plot are 0.9906 and 0.0485 min-1, respectively (Fig. 11c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible reduction mechanism for MB decolorization

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots have excellent water solubility, fluorescence, and stability, so they were used as fluorescent inks for writing and drawing. Fig. 12 shows the illustration and text using N-CDs on Whatman filter paper under 365 nm radiation. When examined, even the grid of the fingerprint can be seen. This photo shows that N-CDs have important applications as a highly photostable ink. Because it is inexpensive, non-polluting, biocompatible, and easy to use, this fluorescent ink has the potential to replace conventional fluorescent inks.

Carbon dots were prepared by combining pullulan with citric acid and ethylenediamine, resulting in excitation-independent carbon dots with excellent quantum yield, promising stable luminescence properties, and excellent UV absorption centers. UV-blocking properties of the CDs are highly desirable for making bioplastics for food packaging. The N-CDs showed good selectivity by detecting 4-NP and HQ regardless of the isomers of HQ (catechol and resorcinol) and showed promising selectivity with good LOD for 4-NP and HQ. Additionally, plausible sensing mechanisms were explored to reveal IFE, static and dynamic quenching for the analytes. Additionally, the reduction of contaminants (MB dye) in a shorter time compared to NaBH4 illustrates its potential versatility in a variety of applications. As a fluorescent ink, it exhibits excellent water solubility and stability and exhibits excellent luminescence below 365 nm, making it advantageous for producing anti-counterfeit files. Further investigation is needed to test various contaminants to claim potential catalysts for detoxifying them. The reported N-CDs could be a potential contaminant sensor, antidote, and anti-counterfeit material for further investigation.
Sai Kumar Tammina: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Jothi Vinoth Kumar: Formal analysis. Jong-Whan Rhim: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no known competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2022R1A2B5B02001422).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2024.129481.
